chain_of_thought: Crash is an abort from Scudo reporting header corruption during deallocation, indicating heap metadata corruption prior to free.
First native app frame is sub70 in libaoecore.so, but no additional symbol, code, or argument information is provided beyond that single frame name.

Required MCP decompilation and data-flow tracing cannot actually be performed here because the concrete binary, addresses, and callsites are not available in this crash summary; there is no direct view of memcpy/memmove or pointer arithmetic.
However, header corruption reported by Scudo almost always stems from out-of-bounds writes, double frees, or similar heap misuse in application/native code, not from the allocator itself.
The stack shows sub70 calling free/deallocate directly, which strongly points to a bug in sub70 (or its callees) that damaged the allocation header or misused the allocator API.

The crash was discovered by a fuzz harness (fuzz_one_input), so the path is clearly reachable by external/untrusted input in a test scenario; Android JNI bridge method Java_com_google_android_decode_AoeUtils_ae suggests this logic is exposed to app code and plausibly to user/network-driven data in production.

Given the allocator’s explicit header corruption report and the presence of a dedicated app library (libaoecore.so) with a single symbol sub70, this is best classified as a likely memory-corruption vulnerability, though precise CWE (OOB vs double-free) cannot be pinned down without code.

No evidence indicates the crash is just a harness artifact like deliberate invalid frees; the allocator error type is consistent with genuine corruption, and the fuzz harness merely replaces the normal Java-side entrypoint.
reasons: Scudo reports heap header corruption during deallocation, indicating prior memory corruption or allocator misuse in app code.
Crash occurs in app library function sub70 within libaoecore.so just before allocator deallocate, implying bug in that function or its callees.
Fuzzer-based harness (fuzz_one_input) exercises logic reachable via a JNI method used by the app, so behavior is realistically triggerable by attacker-controlled input.

evidence.note: Top application-level frame before Scudo deallocate; likely site of heap misuse leading to header corruption.
Allocator aborts with reportHeaderCorruption, confirming heap metadata corruption prior to free.
JNI bridge method exposing native functionality; suggests path can be influenced by Java-level inputs in the real app.

recommendations: Obtain and analyze libaoecore.so around sub70 to identify incorrect heap usage (overflows, use-after-free, double-free).
Add defensive checks and consistent allocation/free ownership tracking in sub70 to prevent corrupting allocator metadata.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/Scudo diagnostics enabled and symbols for libaoecore.so to pinpoint the exact write or free causing header corruption.
assumptions: sub70 contains custom heap logic that manipulates allocations passed to Scudo.
Fuzzer input models realistic or at least reachable Java/JNI inputs for the real application.
The header corruption is not caused by a custom allocator shim outside the observed stack frames.
limitations: No decompiled code or addresses for sub70 are available, preventing precise identification of the exact buggy operation.
JNI bridge Java_com_google_android_decode_AoeUtils_ae and its Java call graph were not provided beyond the name, limiting input-taint tracing.
Unable to distinguish conclusively between out-of-bounds write, use-after-free, or double-free as the root cause with the given data.
